name: Nernst equation for electrochemical cell potentials
principles: free-energy / cell-potential relation (Delta G = -n F E)
conventions: E is the cell (or electrode) potential in volts
E^circ is the standard cell potential (or standard reduction potential), tabulated at 25 deg C with all activities = 1
n is the number of electrons transferred in the balanced redox half-reaction
R = 8.31446 J/(mol K), T in kelvin

Q is the reaction quotient: ratio of activities of products to reactants raised to their stoichiometric coefficients (use concentrations as approximation in dilute solutions; partial pressures for gases)
at T = 298.15 K (25 deg C), the prefactor RT/F = 0.02569 V; combined with ln 10 conversion: E = E^circ - (0.0592 V / n) log10(Q)
expectedLimits: Q = 1 (standard conditions): E = E^circ — operational definition of standard potential
Q -> 0 (products absent): E -> +infinity (reaction strongly favours products, drives forward)
Q -> infinity (reactants absent): E -> -infinity (reaction strongly favours reactants, drives backward)
equilibrium (Delta G = 0): E = 0 and Q = K, the equilibrium constant; Nernst gives ln K = n F E^circ / (R T)
membrane potential limit (concentration cell, E^circ = 0): E = -(RT / nF) ln([X]_in / [X]_out); basis of Nernst potential in physiology
non-ideal electrolytes: replace concentrations by activities (use activity coefficients gamma_i)
references: Nernst (1888) — Die elektromotorische Wirksamkeit der Ionen
Bard & Faulkner — Electrochemical Methods: Fundamentals and Applications, ch.2
IUPAC Gold Book — entry: Nernst equation
